data.title: SDVY: An Outperforming Dividend ETF In The Small- And Mid-Cap Space
data.description:
The First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (NASDAQ:SDVY) offers a refined approach toward dividends. It looks beyond yields by adding dividend growth as a core component of its investment strategy, resulting in a fund with a profile that combines relatively modest yields compared to dividend-focused funds with a strong growth component. SDVY is a fund that is certainly worth keeping on the watchlist as one of the top performers in the category. However, potential investors should be aware of its relatively high volatility and a heavy tilt toward smaller capitalization companies. These are key factors to take into consideration at this moment amid a mixed environment for small caps, including softening growth, but attractive valuation compared to the rest of the market.

ETF Description & Highlights

SDVY provides exposure to U.S. small- and mid-cap companies that have demonstrated consistent dividend growth over recent years. The fund follows a rules-based approach, tracking the Nasdaq US Small Mid Cap Rising Dividend Achievers Index.

This index first screens companies that have increased dividends paid over the trailing twelve months compared to three and five past years. But it does not look only at dividends. Eligible companies must have generated earnings growth over the past three years, the cash-to-debt ratio should be higher than 25%, and the payout ratio below 65%. After passing this first step, companies are assigned a score combining dollar dividend growth over the past five years, higher dividend yield, and lower payout. The purpose of this score is to select the 100 companies best ranked for inclusion in the index. These companies are then equally weighted and rebalanced on a quarterly basis.

As of June 19, 2025, SDVY had $7.9 billion in assets invested in a portfolio of 189 companies, with an average market cap of roughly $7.14 billion. Its allocation is concentrated in small- and mid-caps, as expected, which represent 49.2% and 34.1% of the fund, respectively. For comparison purposes, there is a market-cap weighted ETF, the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), which, despite the name only mentioning mid-caps, also includes a meaningful exposure to small caps, accounting for 68.1% of the fund, while mid-caps make up 34.1%. While both have some similarities in terms of market cap exposure, SDVY has a greater bias toward smaller capitalization companies, with 15.6% of the fund classified as micro caps, and an average market cap that is lower than IJH’s $8.60 billion.

SDVY has a very diluted allocation profile, with its top ten holdings only representing 11% of the fund, and its top holding, InterDigital, weighing 1.2% of the fund. This reflects the fund's equally weighted structure, with slight allocation gaps among constituents being a function of short-term price moves before its quarterly rebalances. Besides InterDigital, other core holdings include Woodward (1.12%), Perdoceo (1.11%), Comfort Systems (1.11%), Cal-Maine (1.1%), Ralph Lauren (1.07%), Paycom (1.06%), CF Industries (1.01%), Unum (1.01%), and Axis Capital (1.0%), spanning sectors like technology, industrials, consumer staples, and financial services.

The table below presents a list of six dividend-oriented ETFs that invest primarily in small- and mid-caps and will serve as the peer group for SDVY in this analysis. The first is the WisdomTree U.S. MidCap Dividend Fund ETF (DON), the largest of the group with over $3 billion in assets under management, following a fundamentally weighted strategy, focused on mid-sized companies that pay regular dividends. Next, the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DES) also focuses on dividend yields with large exposure to small capitalization companies. Meanwhile, the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL) emphasizes dividend consistency, favoring companies with at least 15 consecutive years of dividend increases. The ALPS O’Shares U.S. Small-Cap Quality Dividend ETF Shares (OUSM) adds quality metrics to the dividend orientation, while the ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV) is a subset of the Russell 2000 index and filters small caps with at least 10 years of dividend growth. Completing the list, the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund ETF (DGRS) incorporates quality and growth screens to structure its portfolio.

SDVY’s largest sector allocation by far is to financial services, with 32.4% of total equities, followed by industrials at 24.5% and consumer discretionary at 13.6%. It still has a smaller allocation to consumer staples at 7.2%, technology at 6.6%, basic materials at 6.4%, energy at 4.9%, healthcare at 2.3%, and communication services at 2.1%. The fund has no exposure to real estate or utilities. It is worth noting SDVY’s heavy overweight position in financial services (+15.6%), an above-average yield sector, relative to the benchmark. Meanwhile, it is underweight in the typically low-yield technology sector (–7.9%), but its lower exposure to healthcare (–6.4%) is a bit surprising at first glance, given the relatively high yield in the sector.

If we look at the fund’s portfolio in its largest sectors, we can see that its allocation to financial services is led by banks such as Huntington Bancshares and East West Bancorp, which represent nearly half of the sector exposure. Additional holdings are primarily in the insurance (e.g., Lincoln National and Equitable Holdings), mortgage (Radian Group), and asset management industries in a diversified allocation across the sector.

Industrials is also SDVY’s core sector with most holdings in machinery (e.g., Donaldson, Graco, and ITT) and building products (e.g., Comfort Systems and Tecnoglass), making up roughly 15% of the fund. Remaining positions are spread across construction & engineering, electrical equipment, professional services, and IT services. This makes the fund relatively well-diversified, but still more concentrated in machinery and building products compared to the IJH, its market-cap weighted benchmark.

The fund has an allocation to consumer discretionary in line with the benchmark. Larger exposure is seen in household durables such as Meritage Homes and KB Home. Holdings like Tapestry and Ralph Lauren add exposure to the apparel industry, while Perdoceo and Graham Holdings represent the consumer services segment.

Beyond the top three sectors, SDVY maintains positions in growth sectors such as technology with holdings in software makers InterDigital and Clear Secure, as well as names like Universal Display and Dolby Laboratories. Meanwhile, the fund’s yield focus is reflected in still relevant holdings in a defensive sector like consumer staples through retailers PriceSmart and Andersons, while Cal-Maine Foods and Lancaster Colony add exposure to the food industry.

SDVY’s correlation with peers from a sector exposure perspective has a few key discrepancies. The fund's heavy allocation to financial services (32.4%) is the highest in the group, with most peers, except REGL and SMDV, allocating below 26% to this sector. SDVY’s overweight allocation to industrials (+5.7%) also draws attention, while DON and DES have a more conservative stance to this sector, with nearly 14% exposure. On the flip side, peers maintain significant positions in utilities and real estate, driven by their high yields, while SDVY steers clear of these two sectors. That makes SDVY’s sector correlation sit under 0.90 for most funds, except DGRS, and a weak individual stock overlay below 15% for most funds (DES, REGL, SMDV, and DGRS), which is not bad for investors looking for stock diversification.

High Dividend Growth, Lower Yield

SDVY presents the lowest dividend yield within the peer group, while funds like DES and SMDV yield nearly 3.0%, two funds with a large stance on utilities and real estate, while also sharing a heavy allocation to small caps. On the flip side, SDVY exhibits by far the highest dividend growth profile over the past 5 and 10 years, despite lagging the group over the past year, underscored by the fact that growth plays a key role in its stock selection methodology.

Attractive Valuation, In Line With Peers

SDVY trades at a P/E of 13.4x, representing an 18.7% discount to the benchmark’s 16.4x. The fund value tilt is primarily driven by its heavy allocation to financial services, a typical value sector, but also due to its underweight exposure to technology, where the P/E average is at 20x. Individual stock selection is also a key factor here. SDVY’s valuation discount is in the range of 10% to 20% in most sectors. However, the largest discount occurs in technology, consumer discretionary, and staples, where SDVY’s portfolio P/E is around 40% lower than the benchmark. The fund’s value profile is consistent with its strict stock selection methodology with regard to fundamentals, including cash-to-debt and payout ratio thresholds.

The fund’s growth profile is not limited to dividends; it includes historical earnings growth of 11.3% versus the benchmark’s 10.3%. On top of that, long-term earnings growth of 7.9% is also higher than the 3.9% for the benchmark. Technology, consumer staples, and financial services are key positive contributors at the sector level, more than offsetting slower growth in consumer discretionary and healthcare. However, sales growth lags the benchmark, reflecting SDVY’s underweight stance in the growth-oriented technology sector.

On profitability, SDVY’s EBITDA margin of 23.3% is slightly lower than the benchmark’s 23.7%. This highlights a mixed picture across sectors, with higher margins in technology and financial services not being enough to compensate for weaker numbers in healthcare and energy. On the bright side, ROE is much more favorable for SDVY, averaging 22.2% compared to 16.5% for the benchmark. Operational performance in technology’s holdings is strong at 48.5%, handily surpassing the benchmark, while most sectors show better ROE in the range of 2% to 7%. In summary, while SDVY’s above-average growth profile is consistent with earnings growth measures incorporated in its stock selection methodology, profitability is not the fund’s core factor per se, but is expected to remain at acceptable levels, underscored by a portfolio with companies that have been able to increase dividends over time.

SDVY’s valuation is slightly below the peer group average. Funds like DES (P/E 12.6x) and DGRS (11.6x) trade at low multiples, driven by their low exposure to technology. Meanwhile, SDVY has a more balanced valuation profile, highlighting its overweight exposure to value areas like financial services and a stock-picking methodology that favors higher-growth companies. SDVY’s growth orientation is also illustrated in its historical earnings and sales growth that outpace all peers.

Strong Returns, High Volatility

Dividend funds have struggled to compete with the broader market. The iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), the benchmark for small- and mid-caps in this article, has done better than the peer group of dividend funds, with 3- and 5-year annualized returns of 12.7% and 12.8%, respectively. SDVY comes as an exception in the scenario. Its annualized total returns of 15.7% (3-year) and 16.6% (5-year) have been well ahead of the benchmark. This places SDVY as the best performing fund among peers, outpacing the second and third best funds (OUSM and DON) by more than 2% on an annual basis. This overall outperformance does not mean SDVY did not have setbacks, though. In 2024, for example, it lagged the benchmark, partially eclipsing strong gains in the prior year.

This kind of price action points to a relatively high volatility, which is confirmed by SDVY’s beta of 1.22 and 1.10 over the past three and five years, nearly 5% to 10% above the benchmark, and well above peers, which have seen beta usually under 1.0. SDVY’s capture ratios have been above 100, confirming a tendency to amplify the overall market moves, while other dividend funds have not been able to capture full market upside moves, but proved more resilient during downsides, with some funds showing participation over 100% during market declines.

In summary, SDVY has shown a distinct characteristic compared to dividend-oriented funds. Its above-average volatility is a bit surprising for a dividend fund, but is consistent with the fund’s growth profile, particularly in technology, where most of its holdings have a beta over 1.20.

SDVY’s average daily volume of $53 million is well above peers, who trade under $8 million a day. This leads to low spreads of just 0.05% compared to peers averaging 0.11%. On the other hand, its turnover ratio of 64.0% is the highest in the group, likely driven by its quarterly rebalances, which in turn are reflected in a high expense ratio of 0.59% for a passively managed fund. A weak spot for SDVY and most peers is their tracking error above 10% in most cases. The exception is OUSM, the only fund under 10%, while SDVY shows the worst numbers in the range of 13% to 15%. On risk management, SDVY’s 3- and 5-year Sharpe ratios of 0.35 and 0.69 are the best in the group, alongside OUSM, driven by the fund’s outperformance, offsetting its above-average volatility.

Final Thoughts

SDVY not only delivers on its mandate to offer a portfolio that maximizes dividend growth. It ends up combining strong earnings growth and disciplined valuation. But that comes with a caveat, a somewhat elevated volatility that is not commonly seen in dividend-oriented funds, driven by holdings in growth stocks.

With strong returns over the past years, SDVY positions itself as a fund capable of outperforming the small and mid-cap overall stock market, while offering above-average dividends, though certainly not as high as typical dividend-focused investment vehicles.

On the flip side, the big question is about what lies ahead for smaller capitalization companies. If the outlook remains complex with signs of earnings deceleration, small- and mid-caps are becoming more attractive from a valuation perspective. In this context, investor appetite is likely to favor quality and earnings resilience.

I see it as a positive for dividend funds in general and for SDVY, as it also skews toward dividend resilience and operational performance, given its above-average ROE. However, investors looking for defensiveness may not find SDVY as the best option among dividend funds, given its relatively high volatility and less-than-stellar dividend yields. Thus, I rate SDVY as a hold for value-oriented investors, but see this fund as a good opportunity to outperform the small- and mid-cap dividend funds in the long run.

data.title: Chegg: Activity And Users Plummet (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
To say that the rise of generative AI this year has upended the stock markets would not be an understatement. Beyond tariffs and the macro, the next-largest item on investors' minds that has been moving the markets the year is AI: and while that has been a positive catalyst for many tech stocks, it has also put survival into question for many companies.

Chegg (NYSE:CHGG), in my view, is one of the stocks that is most at risk. The learning and test-prep company, once ubiquitous and popular on college campuses, has seen its share price drop ~25% since the start of the year and ~50% over the past year. If that sounds harsh, it's unfortunately backed by the reality of Chegg's metrics: the company's revenue and user base are declining at similar amounts, and unfortunately, there are few meaningful catalysts that can reverse that decline.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Chegg in January, when the stock was still trading at ~$1.50 per share. Chegg has climbed dramatically from its post-tariff lows and saved itself from delisting: but at the same time, unfortunately, its core metrics have gotten worse. I am downgrading the stock to a sell.

One of the primary reasons that I originally thought Chegg had limited further downside after a sharp fall over the past year was the notion that Chegg would still be able to retain a smaller, but loyal set of users who would be willing to pay the company's monthly subscription fees. This thesis seemed reasonable up until Chegg released disastrous Q1 results, showcasing a deep decline in question activity on the company's apps and a sharp drop-off in paid users. Now, there's little doubt that ChatGPT (even the free, unpaid version, which is tremendously powerful on its own) is essentially making Chegg obsolete.

There are a couple of balls that Chegg has up in the air that are keeping some investors hopeful. The first is content licensing. Over the years, Chegg has built up a valuable "test bank" of question and answer pairs that it has successfully monetized via licensing deals, earning $4 million of revenue in Q1 (or a small ~3% slice of overall revenue).

The addition of licensing revenue streams, plus the ongoing subscription revenue (though declining) from the company's core Study Pack product, is what purportedly has some private equity parties interested in (as shown in the snapshot above, Chegg has cited that it is open to explore "strategic alternatives"). That being said, we find it unlikely that any buyer looking for a multi-year hold on this company will find Chegg's sharp subscriber declines worth rescuing.

All in all, despite Chegg's valiant efforts to stay alive, it is destined to go the way of most technologies: relegated to obsolescence as something better takes its place. The impact of ChatGPT on Chegg's business is clearly evident in the company's results, and I no longer think the company can avoid its eventual dissolution.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Chegg's latest quarterly results in greater detail, which, unfortunately, in my view, accelerated the company's troubles. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Chegg's revenue declined -30% y/y to $121.4 million, beating Wall Street's more modest expectations of $114.6 million (-34% y/y) but decelerating sharply from a -24% y/y decline in Q4 (which, in turn, also decelerated from -13% decline in Q3).

To me, the more telling evidence of Chegg's decline lies in its subscriber metrics. As shown in the chart below, the number of students who pay for Chegg Study Pack declined by 423k sequentially. The company lost 327k payers in the U.S. and 96k overseas, versus "only" a loss of 195k in Q4. The number of overall subscribers declined -31% y/y, to 3.19 million users.

Total retention rates, meanwhile, began to slip even further. Previously, in Q2 and Q3 of last year, despite subscriber declines, retention rates on a monthly basis were actually slightly positive. Since then, however, Q1 showcased a -99bps reduction in retention rates to 81.2%.

Perhaps the most telling negative indicator, however, is the fact that the number of questions asked on the Chegg platform declined -73% y/y to 2.4 million. That is a blatant illustration of the impact that ChatGPT and AI have had on Chegg's usefulness. Chegg's base of knowledge is limited: but ChatGPT's is broad. And though Chegg is also advertising its own ChatGPT-like experience built on generation AI foundations within its site, it doesn't appear to give the company any staying power at all.

Chegg's profitability is also sliding. Adjusted EBITDA cut down by more than half to just $19 million, representing a 16% margin that is 11 points worse y/y. Free cash flow declined by -36% y/y to $16 million.

To be fair, the company is taking aggressive actions to combat its declining profitability. The company is closing more offices and shedding more headcount, per CEO Nathan Schultz's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

As a result, we are once again taking proactive measures to align costs with our business outlook. We executed two restructurings in 2024, and today we are announcing further cost reduction plans. This restructuring will include expense reductions across our business, including closing physical offices in the US and Canada by the end of the year, limiting our upper funnel marketing, reducing new product development efforts, and finally cutting our general and administrative expenses."

Unfortunately, however, cost reductions will hardly be sufficient to combat huge subscriber declines and a massive decline in question activity that is a clear signal of Chegg's eventual obsolescence.

Key takeaways

I no longer have any confidence in Chegg's ability to survive in an AI-dominated world. The company's huge declines in subscribers and Q&A activity on the platform are clear signals that the company has little to offer when ChatGPT is so freely accessible, and younger students who are becoming AI-versed and AI-native will think of Chegg as a very antiquated solution. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Macquarie Healthcare Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global healthcare stocks rose during 1Q25, outperforming the broader equity market.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Healthcare Fund (MUTF:DLHIX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell 3000 Healthcare Index.

The small/mid-cap medical products sector contributed the most to relative performance, driven primarily by stock selection as the sector trailed the broader healthcare Index. The Fund's overweight position in Halozyme Therapeutics Inc. (HALO) was the leading contributor to relative performance within the sector, as the company posted strong earnings and raised guidance. Shares of Intra-Cellular Therapies Inc. also contributed positively to performance after Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced it was acquiring the company. The leading detractor from relative performance in the sector was the Fund's position in Illumina Inc. (ILMN) Shares declined after Chinese regulators added the company to the unreliable entity list, putting about 7% of sales at risk.

An underweight allocation to pharmaceutical contract research and healthcare information technology contributed to relative performance as the sector underperformed the broader market. Healthcare services also contributed positively as shares in CVS Health Corp. (CVS) rallied on higher-than-expected proposed Medicare Advantage rates for 2026 and better-than-expected earnings results. Out-of-benchmark investments also contributed positively, as shares in Federal National Mortgage Association (OTCQB:FNMA) and Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp. (OTCQB:FMCC) rose on anticipation that the Trump administration might reprivatize the entities.

The blue-chip medical products sector detracted somewhat from relative performance mainly due to stock selection. Overweight positions in large pharma companies including Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY), Sanofi SA (SNY), AstraZeneca PLC (AZN), and Gilead Sciences Inc. (GILD) contributed positively as investors rotated into more defensive names. On the negative side, the Fund's holding in UCB SA (OTCPK:UCBJF) detracted from performance as the stock consolidated after a strong run in 2024 despite continued robust prescription trends for its anti-inflammatory drug BIMZELX. The Fund's lack of investment in Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and the underweight position in Johnson & Johnson also detracted from performance as the companies posted strong quarterly sales.

Overall, the biotechnology sector was the largest detractor from performance in the Fund due to its overweight position. The sector underperformed the broader healthcare industry as investors considered the effect changes at the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) would have on the pace of drug approvals. Also, given the rise in economic uncertainty, investors rotated into more defensive companies. The Fund's holdings in Viking Therapeutics Inc. (VKTX) and UniQure NV (QURE) were the largest detractors from performance within the sector. Viking shares declined as investors grew more cautious about obesity drugs in the current regulatory environment. UniQure shares dropped back after more than doubling in 4Q24 on positive regulatory developments for its promising gene therapy treatment for Huntington's Disease. Shares of Regeneron Pharmaceuticals Inc. (REGN) also underperformed as the company faced more competition for its retinal disease treatment Eylea. On the positive side, Alkermes PLC (ALKS) shares contributed positively to performance as the company posted better-than-expected revenues and raised guidance.

Outlook

For global healthcare investors, there are risks that short-term legislative and judicial action could overshadow the positive long-term fundamentals of the healthcare sector and of specific companies. However, we continue to see the potential for long-term opportunities within the global healthcare asset class. The baby-boom generation in the US is aging, implying expanding demand for healthcare products and services for decades to come. At the same time, middle classes in countries with emerging economies (notably India and China) are growing rapidly, creating appetites for Western-style medicine. We remain positive on the healthcare sector and its potential growth opportunities.

We believe that healthcare remains one of the few secular growth sectors in the economy. We continue to see many attractive opportunities, particularly in the biotechnology sector, where we see the potential for continued innovation in cancer treatments and gene therapy technology. We remain overweight in the biotechnology sector, particularly in small- to mid-cap companies, where we see potential for technological breakthroughs.

We continue to put a premium on disciplined, intensive research when analyzing investment opportunities for the Fund. We favor companies that exhibit such traits as proven competitiveness, seasoned management teams, and stock valuations that are discounted meaningfully from our estimates of intrinsic value. These characteristics are part of our daily considerations as we follow our conservative, stock-by-stock approach to portfolio management.

data.title: Macquarie Growth And Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Despite ascending to a new all-time high in mid-February, the broad market S&P Index was down for 1Q25, falling -4.3%, breaking a streak of five quarterly gains. Investors' apparent enthusiasm for the Trump administration's plans for lower taxes and deregulation gave way to broader concerns about the administration's escalating tariff threats and their potential to degrade economic growth. Falling consumer confidence and expectations for higher inflation also appeared to weigh on investor sentiment. In addition, stocks that had been big beneficiaries of the artificial intelligence (AI)-related investing theme came under pressure as the need for huge sums of capital expenditures for AI by US tech companies was called into question. (Source: FactSet.)

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Growth and Income Fund (MUTF:FGIPX) Institutional class shares outperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell Value Index.

At the sector level, an overweight allocation to healthcare contributed to relative performance for the quarter. Healthcare was the Fund's largest sector overweight for the quarter. Within the sector, holdings in CVS Health Corp. (CVS), Gilead Sciences Inc. (GILD), and McKesson Corp. (MCK), were the key contributors. McKesson is the nation's largest pharmaceutical distributor to hospitals, retail pharmacies, and medical offices. On the earnings front, McKesson reported third-quarter fiscal 2025 earnings, showing revenue growth (to billion) and increased per-share earnings guidance of up to year-over-year growth. In addition, the company also announced the acquisition of a national eye care company, which boosts the company's overall care capabilities.

An overweight allocation to the financials sector was a key detractor from relative performance for the quarter, driven by weak stock selection. At the stock level, a key detractor was a holding in Evercore Inc. (EVR), a global investment banking advisory firm. Despite 4Q24 earnings and revenue-beating estimates, a reported increase in expenses detracted from the company's results, with total expenses increasing 14.4% year on year. Other financial holdings that detracted from Fund performance include PNC Financial Services Group Inc. (PNC) and Invesco Ltd. (IVZ)

Outlook

Uncertainty surrounding trade policy is high, particularly as we ended the quarter and edged closer to "Liberation Day," the anticipated date for implementing broad tariffs. Indeed market return dynamics look different from the last period of trade policy uncertainty. This, along with some cooling of macroeconomic data points, could add to market volatility but the range of potential outcomes presents an opportunity for actively managed strategies that can take advantage of higher dispersion in company outcomes as firms adapt to policy change with varying levels of uncertainty.

data.title: Macquarie Global Listed Real Assets Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Financial markets experienced significant fluctuations in 1Q25, influenced by escalating inflation concerns and policy uncertainties stemming from the Trump administration's trade strategies. Overall, global fixed income outperformed global equities as market volatility, driven by policy uncertainty, led to a general risk-off environment.

Global capital markets began 2025 on a mixed but generally positive note; however, volatility increased as the quarter progressed, driven by macroeconomic uncertainty, geopolitical tensions, and shifting monetary policy expectations.

January saw solid gains in equities, especially in European markets, while US stocks lagged slightly. Optimism about artificial intelligence boosted sentiment early in the month, although the Chinese market faced pressure after DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), a Chinese AI firm, introduced a cheaper and more efficient model that triggered selloffs in tech and energy. Chinese equities fell sharply on weak economic data across gross domestic product (GDP), trade, and industrial output.

February brought heightened political tension and trade uncertainty. A proposed tariff on Canadian and Mexican imports - later enacted only for another month - alongside new tariffs on Chinese goods, stirred market unease. Still, bonds benefited from the risk-off sentiment, and Japanese and US equities saw modest gains. Inflation remained persistent, with the US Consumer Price Index remaining sticky, partly due to higher oil prices and tighter sanctions on Russia.

March was marked by a sharp pickup in volatility. US President Trump's trade policies escalated global market concerns, with new tariffs announced on EU imports and retaliatory threats further clouding sentiment. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) meeting on March 19 reinforced these concerns. Although the Fed left interest rates unchanged, Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasized increased uncertainty around economic projections, while removing the portion from previous statements that risks to employment and inflation "are balanced." The Fed's updated forecasts included upward revisions in near-term inflation expectations as well as lower median GDP growth and higher unemployment in 2025, clearly signaling a cautious stance. Big tech stocks struggled while US retail sales, consumer sentiment, and industrial production softened. The result was a broad-based market pullback, especially in US equities.

Currency markets reflected shifting risk appetite: the US dollar weakened, the Japanese yen appreciated, and the Russian ruble rebounded as geopolitical tensions eased slightly.

In summary, 1Q25 offered early optimism that gave way to uncertainty. Persistent inflation, mixed economic signals, and a sharp rise in trade tensions weighed on sentiment by quarter-end, leaving investors cautious heading into 2Q25.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Global Listed Real Assets Fund (MUTF:DPREX) outperformed the Fund's primary benchmark, the Bloomberg Global Inflation-Linked Total Return Index Value Hedged USD. Infrastructure equities and commodities were the main drivers of this outperformance.

We also compare the Fund against a blended real assets index consisting of 20% global natural resources, 20% global real estate, 20% global listed infrastructure, and 40% global inflation-linked bonds. For 1Q, the Fund slightly underperformed this reference index. The allocation effects contributed to relative performance against this index, while security selection detracted slightly.

At the sector level, the Fund's investments in energy and materials detracted from performance, while industrials contributed. Within energy, oil, gas, and consumable fuels detracted from performance while within materials, chemicals also detracted. Within industrials, transportation and capital goods drove outperformance.

We periodically examine the contribution of derivatives to the Fund's performance. Based on the available information, the Fund's derivative positions had no material impact on performance.

Portfolio positioning

The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing in listed real asset securities and may purchase both equity and fixed income securities. The Fund seeks diversification among real asset classes in terms of both geography and asset class.

During the quarter, we may adjust our exposures to reflect our convictions based on the changing economic environment. Accordingly, no tactical adjustments were made to the Fund during the quarter. We will continue to monitor the economic environment and will adjust our exposures accordingly based on our continued investment and risk management philosophy and process.

Outlook

Amid escalating trade tensions and policy uncertainties, we maintain a cautious outlook on capital markets. The US economy remains stable but shows signs of a slowdown, with GDP growth projections revised downward for the year. While corporate earnings are generally positive, equity valuations remain elevated despite recent market corrections. The Fed's projection of increased inflation, partly due to tariff impacts, adds complexity to the investment landscape. Given these factors, we remain cautiously optimistic that a resilient economic environment and robust corporate earnings might support further gains for US equities while the outlook for Europe and emerging markets remain mixed. On the other hand, volatility in long-term interest rates present challenges for fixed income.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Macquarie Global Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Economic growth in the US slowed further in 1Q25, continuing a trend that began in the second half of 2023. The slowdown became more apparent during the quarter, possibly reflecting consumer and business caution due to rising uncertainty around the Trump administration's policy agenda (both in terms of trade policies and government spending cuts). The US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its restrictive monetary policies, which have continued to weigh on growth, although fiscal policy has remained supportive. Consumer spending slowed somewhat, and while this followed robust spending in 4Q24, a cooling labor market could cause consumers to remain cautious in the months ahead. Beyond this, while tariffs are likely to weigh on US growth, a resumption in the Fed's current easing cycle, along with renewed fiscal stimulus, should provide a material positive offset for economic activity in the medium term.

On inflation, the Core Consumer Price Index (Core CPI) again saw a significant increase in January - repeating the start-of-year pattern of recent years - although this was followed by a more subdued reading in February. The Fed's preferred Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation measure indicated that inflation has been better contained over recent months, although the February reading (in contrast to the CPI) was elevated. The more modest pace of demand, ongoing supply expansion, and a labor market that continues to soften should maintain the broader downward trend in inflation. Even so, the path towards the Fed target looks to be slower and bumpier than was earlier anticipated. Labor demand has continued to cool as hiring trends slow, and labor supply has continued to grow, even as immigration has declined. With the resulting better balance in the labor market, wage growth to continue to ease. While tariffs should boost prices in the coming months, we believe that is likely to have only a one-off impact, especially with most measures of inflation expectations remaining well anchored. Even this one-off impact will likely be tempered by the negative impact tariffs may have on both global and US activity.

Outside the US, euro-zone data continued to point toward subdued economic activity growth in that region, while Chinese growth seemed to benefit somewhat from the stimulus measures announced in 2024. In Europe, the German economic outlook remains concerning in the near term, with structural headwinds buffeting key industries including automobiles and chemicals. Further downside risks stem from the US administration's trade policies. While these all point to downside risks in coming months, we believe a substantially more supportive fiscal outlook will boost growth beyond this with significant government spending on defense and infrastructure now planned over the next few years. Given the degree of slack currently present in the German economy, we do not anticipate materially higher inflation. The European Central Bank (ECB), after cutting rates by 100 basis points in 2024, followed this up with additional 25 basis-point rate cuts in January and March (one basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point). An additional two-and-a-half 25 basis-point cuts over the remainder of 2025 are now priced into rates markets.

The sharp rise in German bond yields that we saw following the recent fiscal-spending announcement serves as a reminder that fiscal outlooks remain a potential cause for concern in many European countries. Europe also remains susceptible to geopolitical events that can affect energy security, with the Russia-Ukraine war continuing to be a potential source of inflationary concern, along with ongoing tension in the Middle East. While the stimulus measures announced by a range of Chinese policymakers over recent months are helping to stabilize activity in the near term, the structural headwinds facing the Chinese economy remain strong, especially the ongoing and deepseated problems in the property sector. In contrast to most other major central banks, economic conditions in Japan, especially wage and inflation outcomes, are pushing the Bank of Japan (BoJ) to further increase rates as it continues to move away from its previous negative-rate policies. After raising rates in January, the rates markets expect a further increase in the months ahead.

While the higher bond yields at the start of 2025 provided attractive entry levels, the subsequent decline in yields since then suggests a more cautious stance is warranted. We will continue to consider increasing duration as yields rise but also reduce exposure when the market looks rich. In addition, geopolitical considerations such as the new US administration's policies are increasingly affecting bond yields, and the response of other countries to these policies will also be important. On credit, while the risk aversion we saw in markets has caused spreads to widen somewhat, this comes from a historically tight starting point. While risks to growth have increased, the prospect of further policy support (both monetary and fiscal) is likely to benefit the medium-term growth outlook. Moreover, while spreads remain relatively tight despite the recent widening, all-in yields remain high relative to recent history and expectations of positive total returns should drive demand.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Global Bond Fund (MUTF:IVSIX) Institutional Class shares modestly underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index, Hedged to USD, on an after-fees basis.

Interest rate duration positioning slightly detracted from performance, though this was offset by curve positioning. The Fund held an overweight to European duration, which detracted from performance after the German election, and a significant step up in spending commitments and discarding of the debt brake. Curve positioning was positive across almost all regions, as global curves steepened: Slower growth expectations lowered short-end yields (with expectations for renewed rate cuts), while the long end remained under pressure due to lingering inflation concerns, and expectations for more global government spending.

The Fund's credit positioning produced a flat result versus the benchmark, with renewed volatility in global credit markets in the latter half of the quarter. Sector allocations to investment grade and emerging markets were detractors, but in both cases, this was offset by positive security selection. We added exposure to high yield late in the quarter, amid sharp volatility in the asset class and more attractive spreads. The high yield sector was a detractor over the quarter given the significant widening in higher-beta credit spreads.

We increased the Fund's interest rate duration modestly over the quarter, moving to a peak of 6.50 years by quarter end, from a starting point of 6.20 years. During the quarter, the main additions were in Australian, US, and Japanese duration, as yields rebounded from an early March rally. We trimmed European duration, given the significant change in stance from European governments on spending and defense. We still view neutral rates in Europe as lower than the level broadly priced by the market, but in the short term there is heightened uncertainty on the outlook.

The Fund's currency exposures remain heavily weighted to the US dollar, given the generally hedged nature of the strategy. We view the dollar as likely past its peak valuation for this cycle.

We expect continued volatility in fixed income markets and an increasing focus on global growth amidst escalating trade tensions. Investors are focused on the achievement of a "soft landing" in economies, where inflation is brought back to target without a period of sharply sub-trend growth, but recent volatility and changes in global government spending patterns threaten to make that landing more difficult. The risk remains that the glide path will be disrupted, either by an intensifying weakness in the labor market, sticky inflation, or tension driven by heavy government bond supply. Therefore, our overall stance is to gradually increase duration and find pockets of value in credit markets to add to the Fund's portfolio. The Fund has a significant ability to increase its credit holdings, and we will look to do so as potential opportunities emerge.

Outlook

In 1Q25, investors shifted their focus to the potentially deleterious effects of Trump's policies, including tariff wars, federal layoffs, and spending cuts. As a result, markets pulled back from historically rich valuations. Consumer and business sentiment was noticeably weaker, even as hard data remained resilient. Clarity around the scope of potential changes would help assuage market concerns but that may take some time to emerge. Meanwhile, there appears to be some agreement between market participants and the Fed that there will likely be two further cuts this year. That said, the potential for unexpected developments in the economy and the Fed's reaction remains a concern for us.

Our strategy remains tilted to the defensive, with higher levels of interest rate duration, and relatively low levels of overall credit exposures. We tactically added some risk in the recent bout of market volatility but remain at the low end of risk historically.

data.title: Wall Street Weekend: Markets Have Been Looking For An Excuse To Rally
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Economic expert Danielle DiMartino Booth on how markets have been looking for an excuse to rally (0:30). Whatever you do, don't lose your job! (1:25) Jay Powell, weakening housing market and fighting inflation (4:25). US dollar behaving counterintuitively (8:22). This is an excerpt from a recent Investing Experts episode.

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Danielle DiMartino Booth, always a pleasure to have you on Seeking Alpha. Thanks for joining us. Really appreciate it.

Danielle DiMartino Booth: Well, it's great to be here. We need to do this more often.

RS: We absolutely do. I agree. We should not wait as long between appearances. Speaking of waiting between appearances, a lot has been going on in the world.

Talk to us about how you are thinking about things, processing things, waiting for things. What's your take on the state of the world, the state of the markets right now?

DDB: Markets have been looking for an excuse to rally. And once that mind frame is set, then it won't matter what happens.

Good news, bad news. Markets are momentum flow-driven beasts, and they have done a very good job of absolutely dismissing and ignoring whatever is happening in the underlying economy.

And as investors, we should be very cognizant of what makes markets hum and what's happening in the real economy because they're two completely separate prisms into the world.

RS: So what are you looking at in the real economy? What are the data points that you're most concentrated on and looking for?

DDB: So I think right now, one of the biggest takeaways if you're above the sky flying at 40,000 feet is, don't lose your job. Whatever you do, do not lose your job.

There was a trend with the Atlanta Fed. I don't like the Atlanta Fed's GDP now tracker. I think it's a Mickey Mouse model. I shouldn't be disparaging. But the Atlanta Fed's wage growth tracker, that's something I follow really closely.

And for years after the pandemic hit, if you were looking for greener pastures, if you were a job switcher, you were getting paid up big time to go job hop, job to job to job.

The last three months, we've seen that dynamic flipped on its head. So if you're a job stayer, these are two different terms the Atlanta Fed uses, job switcher, job stayer.

Right now, you're getting paid less if you're quitting your job. You're getting paid more if you stay in it. Why is that? Because companies are putting a premium on reliability.

Companies actually want to see their employees back in the office. It's companies right now that are in the driver's seat, and you have to pay attention to that because it's a narrative shift.

And that shows you don't lose your job because it's really hard to find one when you've had nearly 2 million Americans as continuing jobless claimants and the highest levels that we've seen in years multiple, it's telling you something.

That when the quits rate is going down - this used to be Janet Yellen's favorite job market indicator because it was to take this job and shove it rate.

If you can't quit your job right now because job insecurity is going up, which is the opposite of what happened in the aftermath of the pandemic, that's telling you something about the underlying health of the US economy.

The only thing that we can rely on is the unreliability of the data.

Because of the revisions that I was speaking about months ago, it simply become more entrenched. The downward revisions are deeper than they've ever been in US history.

And that tells you that markets are gonna key off of 'nonfarm payrolls grew by this much.' Are bond traders, are stock jocks, are they gonna trade off of that headline figure? Yes. They are.

But the actual underlying data, because of the magnitude and the systematic nature of these downward revisions becoming deeper and deeper with each quarterly revision, by the way, to the Bureau of Labor Statistics model, compared to the 12 million American companies that by law must report head counts once a quarter to the Census Bureau.

That's actual hard data on the ground.

The divide between the models at the Bureau of Labor Statistics and what's actually happening has never been wider. Trade off of it.

And as I say, my mantra has never ever applied more. Trade the narrative, own the truth.

RS: Another thing that we talked about last time you were on was Powell and interest rates and fighting inflation. Lots of jawboning since then.

What are your thoughts about the Fed, about Powell's role at the Fed, about fighting inflation and the interest rate conversation? What are your thoughts there, and what would you say you're looking towards this summer and the next couple of meetings?

DDB: I think one of the biggest changes in the US economy is the rapidity with which we've seen the housing market weaken. Nobody was expecting this. This is completely out of left field.

We had S&P Case Shiller home prices decline for a second month in April. March and April, this is peak spring selling season, and you're seeing home prices month-over-month decline two months in a row?

What does this have to do with Jay Powell? Guess what? Housing's the biggest input to the CPI. Housing's the biggest input to the PCE.

If home prices are falling during peak home selling season, and we know that May was weaker than March and April, that means you're gonna have three months in a row of falling home prices. Pay attention, Chair Powell.

It's gonna be a lot more meaningful, and it's gonna be a much greater drag on inflation that home prices are falling for the first time since '05, '06, '07, that you need to shift your focus away from being hyper focused on tariffs and the potential for bleed through when the largest input to inflation has got a negative sign in front of it.

That's going to overwhelm any type of, oh, tariffs might cause inflation, concerns that Powell is voicing, making it appear that he might be making monetary policy against Donald Trump, which is a big no no.

The Fed's an apolitical, independent institution. It is Jay Powell's mandate to make monetary policy in the public interest and not just be doing things because the President is calling him a loser or whatever he's saying in his non-presidential way.

It is Jay Powell's duty to make monetary policy regardless of what's happening in the political arena, and the hard inputs to inflation and the hard inputs to the labor metrics are telling him that monetary policy is restrictive.

It's too tight.

RS: So what's your sense of what's gonna come from the next couple of meetings? Are you encouraged by what you think might be coming? Are you discouraged by it?

DDB: I'm encouraged by the fact that we have not one, not two, but three Fed officials who've come out after the latest Fed decision and say, you know what? July should be in the cards here. We're seeing enough on the labor side of our mandate that we shouldn't be talking about September right now.

Because if we're talking about September, that means that the economy probably needs it right now, and that July should be a live meeting.

So I'm happy that you're seeing enough dissenting voices in order to shift the narrative away from we can just wait, we can sit on our hands, we have plenty of time, we have all the flexibility in the world.

That doesn't look like it's the case anymore, but I'm happy to hear Fed officials come out and say it out loud.

You make chair Powell's life a little bit uncomfortable. You know what? He won't make that same mistake by pausing in July as he did in 2024 and waiting for an unusually large rate cup to come through in September.

Let's not wait. Let's go ahead and deliver to the US economy. And by the way, 25 basis points, it's just a pimple on the you know what of humanity, but it's still the Fed signaling. You know what?

I'm signaling to say, I hear you, American working people. I hear you that you're struggling. I wanna signal that I'm with you, not against you. It's enough sometimes for the markets and for the economy to get that signal.

RS: And what would you say about the dollar (DXY) these days? How are you thinking about that? How are you looking at it in the short term and long term?

DDB: So it's interesting because we're guiding our clients right now to behave as counterintuitively as the greenback has behaved.

So when the Fed goes into a holding pattern, vis a vis the rest of the world and the rest of the world central banks are in an easing posture, and we see the dollar fall out of bed, well, I mean, the contrarian mind in me thinks, okay, fine.

So if we're tighter relative to the rest of the world and the dollar falls, maybe when the Fed starts to lower interest rates, the dollar is going to counterintuitively rally because it's not been following a script at all, and it's one of those crowded trades out there is we wanna sell the dollar, we wanna sell the dollar.

Well, what happens if the Fed starts to lower rates and we see the dollar rally? It can happen.

And that's actually how we're guiding our clients into the second half of 2025 is to anticipate a counter trend dollar rally because the dollar has been weakening even as monetary policy here has been tighter relative to the rest of the world's central banks.

I'm a contrarian in my thinking, part of that has to do with the fact that we've seen services disappoint in Germany. We've seen services disappoint in China.

This working assumption that if the United States catches a cold, the rest of the world's not gonna get pneumonia, it's never been proven in the history of mankind.

We are the largest economy by a wide margin on the planet, and if our economy is slowing enough to make the Fed start to cut interest rates, you know what?

It's going to have ripple effects in the rest of the world economy that we've already seen is slowing despite their central banks already being in an easing framework.

data.title: Delek Logistics Partners: Steady Cash Flow And Strategic Expansion Amid High Leverage
data.description:
Executive Summary

Delek Logistics (NYSE: DKL) is a Permian-focused midstream MLP that combines a high-yield distribution with growth from recent expansions. In Q1 2025, DKL delivered record adjusted EBITDA of $116.5 million (up 15% YoY) and distributable cash flow (DCF) of $75.1 millions. The partnership just declared a 49th consecutive quarterly distribution, raising it to $1.110 per unit (a 0.5% increase sequentially), which yields ~10.3% at current prices. Debt is high (about $2.15 billion) relative to equity, but steady cash flow helps cover payments. I believe the stock’s low valuation (≈14.5× trailing EPS and ~11× EV/EBITDA) partly reflects elevated leverage and execution risk, but it offers an attractive yield cushion. Over time, growth from higher Permian volumes, water and gas projects, and further parent deconsolidation could lift DKL’s intrinsic value.

Company Overview and Background

Delek Logistics Partners, LP is a midstream energy MLP headquartered in Brentwood, Tennessee. It owns pipelines, processing plants and storage primarily in the Permian Basin (both Midland and Delaware areas) and the Gulf Coast. The company provides gathering, pipeline, transportation and related services for crude oil, NGLs/intermediates, refined products, natural gas, and produced water. Major assets include the Libby and Tanya gas processing plants in the Delaware Basin, extensive crude gathering and truck terminals in the Midland, and various legacy pipeline JVs. The business is organized into four segments: Gathering & Processing (gas/oil plants and pipelines), Wholesale Marketing & Terminalling (fuel terminals and trading), Storage & Transportation (tank/storage and crude pipelines), and Pipeline Joint Ventures (equity stakes in pipelines).

Financial Health

Delek Logistics is the midstream arm of Delek US, with assets concentrated in the Delaware and Midland Basins. Its Q1-2025 financial results highlight solid cash generation and controlled leverage. Adjusted EBITDA was $116.5 million, up from $101.5 million a year ago, thanks to acquisitions (Gravity and H2O water systems) and rising throughput. DCF as adjusted was $75.1 million, covering distributions by ~1.27×. Free cash flow remains thin due to heavy capex (Libby 2 gas plant expansion) and unit buybacks, but operating cash flow improved relative to last year.

On the balance sheet, total debt is $2.15 billion vs. virtually no cash (≈$2.1 million), giving net debt/EBITDA above 4×. Shareholders’ equity is minimal ($97 million book value) so book leverage ratios (debt/equity) look extreme; but in practice DKL funds most capex by debt or equity issuance (it raised $165 million from new units in 2024). Interest coverage is modest; full-year 2024 interest expense ($150 million) was about 60% of operating income (EBIT). In short, DKL has leveraged up to grow, but Q1 results show healthy cash flow and distribution coverage above 1.2×. The partnership’s liquidity is supported by a $1.15 billion credit facility (nearly $445 million undrawn) and ongoing unit issuances for acquisitions.

Financial Performance and Peer Comparison

Over the trailing twelve months (TTM through Q1’25), DKL generated $938 million in revenue and $148 million in net income (≈15.9% profit margin). Its EBITDA was ~$275 million (≈29% EBITDA margin). In Q1-2025 alone, DKL’s performance was strong: revenue grew slightly (due in part to acquisitions) while adjusted EBITDA jumped ~15% YoY to $116.5 million. Gross margin (EBITDA/revenue) expanded as fixed costs were spread over higher volumes.

By segment (Q1 vs Q1-2024): Gathering & Processing EBITDA rose sharply to $81.1 million from $57.8 million, driven by the added Midland Water assets and higher throughput. Wholesale Marketing & Terminalling fell to $17.8M (from $25.3M) as fuel and terminalling margins eased. Storage & Transportation EBITDA dropped to $14.5M (from $18.1M) due to renegotiated contracts. Pipeline Joint Ventures (equity income) was $10.2M (versus $8.5M prior) thanks to new W2W pipeline contributions. Corporate costs were a small loss (~$6.9M), slightly less negative than last year. In sum, the Gathering segment is fueling growth, offsetting some cyclical weakness in refining-related transport.

On a per-unit basis, 2024 net income of $148.3 million equated to $2.98 diluted EPS (TTM). The distribution of $1.11 per quarter yields about 37% of this ($4.44 annual). Much of the remaining cash flow is plowed back into capex (Libby expansion, etc.) or reducing debt. DKL’s ROE and ROIC are distorted by its tiny equity base (the Yahoo stats show ROE >500% due to negative book), but operational returns are modest – consistent with midstream peers that are infrastructure-heavy. The balance sheet is leveraged: net debt is roughly 4.2× EBIT (and over 7× on a cash-flow basis), meaning interest costs (~$150M/year) consume ~40% of EBITDA.

Ratio (TTM) DKL NEXT GEL HAFN STNG INSW
EV / EBITDA ~11.1× ~18.9× ~10.0× ~3.9× ~3.2× ~4.4×
EV / Revenue ~4.75× — ~1.77× ~1.30× ~2.34× ~2.68×
Price / Sales ~2.28× — ~0.73× ~0.99× ~1.89× ~2.14×
Price / Book ~23.6× ~7.5× ~6.75× ~1.11× ~0.71× ~0.98×

DKL’s EV/EBITDA (~11×) is higher than some of their peers but lower than NEXT. Its EV/Revenue(~4.8×) is well above most peers, reflecting that DKL’s asset base is relatively lean for its revenue.

Valuation and Fair Value Estimation

DKL now trades at roughly $42–43 per unit (June 2025), implying a market cap ~$2.3 billion. At ~$43, the annual $4.44 distribution yields about 10.3%. This high yield is the primary attraction for investors, equivalent to a ~13.4% yield on book equity (though book is near zero). Trailing P/E is ~14.5× (TTM EPS ~$2.98). Given the distribution coverage >1×, the yield may be considered sustainable if volumes hold.

Comparatively, midstream pipelines with similar growth profiles typically trade at 12–15× P/E or EV/EBITDA in the high-teens. DKL’s ~11× EV/EBITDA sits at the low end of that range. Thus, on an absolute basis, DKL doesn’t look deeply cheap, but its 10%-plus yield is rare in energy infrastructure. If one values DKL by discounted cash flows or relative yield, the stock could be justified in the low-$40s. For example, if DKL sustains flat distribution growth and ~5% EBITDA growth over 5 years, then at a ~9–10% required return the stock value is roughly today’s price. Any longer-term upside likely depends on deleveraging or above-trend volume growth.

Analysts are mixed but generally positive. Public consensus targets have been around $43–44, roughly where it trades now. Some buy-side analysts argue the pipeline sector could re-rate up if macro instability subsides, giving modest upside. (For reference, NextDecade is worth ~$8 with no earnings and speculative LNG prospects, while shipping peers have volatility. In that context, DKL’s cash flows and backlog feel more stable.)

In short, DKL’s current valuation assumes limited growth beyond the generous yield. If results beat guidance (e.g. higher throughput or accretive deals), the stock could rerate modestly. Even a small move to 15× P/E (implying ~$45–46) would not be an aggressive multiple in midstream. Conversely, if oil/gas prices or drilling decline, coverage could dip below 1×, so investors are already demanding a big margin of safety (hence the 10% yield). A fair 12–14× EV/EBITDA on steady-state cash flow (versus the current 11×) might suggest a base-case target in the mid-$40s.

Key Risks and Challenges

Commodity and Volume Risk

DKL’s cash flows depend on Permian production. A steep oil or gas price drop could lead producers to cut drilling plans, reducing midstream volumes. While Permian breakevens are low, sustained sub-$60 WTI might eventually slow growth. Demand for disposal water also hinges on fracking activity; any shift to more water recycling (vs disposal) could hurt that business.

Interest Rate/Leverage

Over $2 billion of debt matures by ~2028, mostly at floating rates. Higher interest rates inflate interest expense (2024 interest was ~$152 million ~13% of revenue) and could pressure distributions if cash flow lags. Refinancing risk is real, and a 100bp hike adds ≈$20M/yr expense.

Integration and Execution

DKL’s growth strategy relies on successfully building projects (e.g. Libby plant expansion, AGI wells) and integrating acquisitions (H2O, Gravity). Cost overruns or delays could defer cash flows. Mixing water and crude systems adds complexity; any operational issues (like water pipeline leaks or gas plant upsets) could dent earnings.

Conclusion

Delek Logistics offers a compelling risk-reward for yield-oriented investors. It is a well-capitalized Permian midstream operator with diversified assets in oil, gas and water flow. Its key positives are (1) a strong pipeline of growth projects (Libby expansion, water systems) and an improving revenue mix (now ~80% third-party); (2) a very high and rising distribution (10%-yielding and recently hiked for the 49th quarter); and (3) management’s emphasis on deleveraging after expansion. On the other hand, substantial leverage and commodity cyclicality limit upside. The market is essentially pricing DKL as a high-yield, slow-growth midstream: not a high-flyer, but one that pays you while waiting.

For a long-horizon investor, the case would be attractive if one believes Permian volumes will continue growing steadily and that management will succeed in modestly trimming debt or boosting EBITDA faster than expected. In that scenario, even a small re-rating (to 12–13× EV/EBITDA) or improvement in distribution growth could yield gains beyond the ~2–3% premium to peers now. Conversely, if oil/gas markets sag or inflation strains distribution coverage, the stock may move sideways or down.

DKL is a distribution-rich midstream play with solid cash flows and concrete growth projects, currently trading at a subdued valuation that reflects its risks. Investors with patience for energy-cycle swings may find the ~10% yield and leverage-downside cushion compelling, especially if the partnership can steadily grow EBITDA toward its $500 million guidance. In our view, DKL is fairly priced for now – neither a screaming bargain nor a sell – but leans toward a cautious buy for those willing to bet on the Permian outlook and management’s de-risking plan.

data.title: Macquarie Floating Rate Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index returned 0.48% in 1Q25, as concerns about tariffs, volatility across capital markets, and economic policy uncertainty generally weighed on the asset class. Performance in January was strong but as the quarter progressed, a shift in sentiment led to March being the first negative month for total returns since October 2023. Overall, CCC-rated loans underperformed during the quarter, with a decline of 0.57% compared to gains of 0.93% for BB-rated loans and 0.35% for B-rated loans.

The technical picture softened somewhat during the quarter with an increase in financial market volatility. Inflows to retail loan funds slowed to $1.35 billion while collateralized loan obligation (CLO) issuance of $48.55 billion remained robust. Activity in the new-issue market remained heavy, secured by a busy January. Gross supply during the quarter was the fourth highest on record at $351 billion, of which 53% was for repricing activity and 18% was for refinancing.

Fundamentals continued to show resilience during the quarter. For public filers within the Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index, 4Q24 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) increased 2% year over year, the 17th straight positive reading dating back to 4Q20. The Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index default rate finished 4Q24 at 0.82%, compared with the historical average of 3%.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Floating Rate Fund (MUTF:DDFLX) Institutional Class shares slightly underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index.

Contributors to performance included:

  • An overweight to and security selection within the financial services sector
  • An underweight to the household and personal products sector
  • Security selection within the food, beverage, and tobacco sector and the materials sector
  • The Fund's positions in collateralized loan obligation (CLO) debt securities

Detractors from performance included:

  • An underweight to the real estate management and development sectors
  • An underweight to and security selection within the media and entertainment sector
  • An underweight to and security selection within the consumer services sector
  • Security selection within the automobiles and components and telecommunication services sectors

Outlook

Risks to loan market issuers remain as economic policy uncertainty has increased, which could weigh on growth, both domestically and globally. While geopolitical risks remain elevated, interest rates remain high, and inflation is stubbornly persistent, we believe fundamentals should generally prove resilient for most issuers, as years of issuer friendly capital markets have allowed for robust levels of refinancing. As a result, balance sheets are generally healthy, and we believe default expectations remain manageable.

However, we believe credit selection will likely be critical to navigating an environment in which there will be winners and losers. Default rates are expected to be in the 2.0% to 3.0% range over the next 12 months, and we are positioning our portfolios accordingly. We believe this approach to positioning allows investors to carry a competitive yield while cushioning volatility resulting from the changing economic environment.

Sources: LCD, an offering of S&P Global Market Intelligence, and S&P Structured Finance Group.
